coredata.dc:title: NGQDs modified 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheet heterojunctions with large interfacial contact area for high-performance supercapacitor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaojie
Lai, Hanlin
Lu, Huiling
Zhou, Pengjie
Ying, Yulong
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The structural and compositional optimization in nanomaterials effectively improves their capacity characteristics. Herein, we present nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) modified NiCo-layered double hydroxides (LDH)/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets heterojunctions with well-designed hierarchical structures. The novel two-dimensional (2D)/2D multi-component structure is strategically developed to eliminate agglomeration and layer stacking, thereby facilitating the insertion and desorption of ions in electrochemical processes. Meanwhile, the in-situ formed NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 heterojunction significantly diminishes charge transport resistance and boosts the electrochemical reaction rate. Besides, the incorporated NGQDs, known for their excellent conductivity and abundant active sites, further enhance the ability of electronic conductivity and charge storage dynamics of the electrode materials. The resultant composite electrode shows an impressive specific capacity of 1832.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, coupled with a commendable energy density of 68.9 Wh kg−1 when the power density is 800 W kg−1 in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//activated carbon supercapacitors. Finally, the device demonstrates superior cyclic stability, and its capacity can still be maintained at the original 91.5 % during 10,000 cycles of charge and discharge. These exemplary results underscore the potential of 2D/2D heterogeneous interfaces in revolutionizing the field of energy storage, particularly by enhancing electrical conductivity and charge storage capabilities, paving the way for developing advanced energy storage systems with optimized nanomaterial interfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
Multi-component
Hierarchical structure
Heterojunction
Two-dimensional nanosheet

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram illustrating the synthetic method for CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs.
(a) XRD pattern of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. (b-f) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, Ni 2p, Co 2p, O 1s and N 1s.
SEM images of (a-c) CC@Co-MOF, (d-f) CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 and (g-i) CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs.
(a,b) TEM images and (c) HRTEM image of the NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. (d) Typical TEM image and the corresponding element distribution.

(a, b) Compared CV and GCD curves of CC@Co-MOF, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs were at 2 mV s− 1 and 1 A g−1, respectively. (c-d) CV curves and GCD curves of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. (e) Column comparison diagram of specific capacitance of three electrode materials. (f) Nyquist diagram of each electrode material obtained by analog circuit.

(a) CV curves and the corresponding linear fit line graph (b) for CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode. (c) Separation of the capacitive distribution at 2, 3, 4 and 5.0 mV s−1. (d) Capacitive and diffusion control ratio. (e-g) CV curves of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs, and the linear fitting curves of sweep rate and capacitance current.

(a) Assembly sketch map of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC supercapacitor. (b) CV curves (2 mV s−1) of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs positive and AC negative electrodes. (c, d) CV test curve of the assembled supercapacitor at a given potential window and at a conventional scanning rate. (e) GCD curves and corresponding calculated capacity.

(a) Ragone plots, (b) the practical application and (c) cycle performance and coulombic efficiency of the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC supercapacitor. (d) SEM image of the ternary electrode after cycling.

head.ce:title: NGQDs modified 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheet heterojunctions with large interfacial contact area for high-performance supercapacitor

Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, 310018, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The structural and compositional optimization in nanomaterials effectively improves their capacity characteristics. Herein, we present nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) modified NiCo-layered double hydroxides (LDH)/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets heterojunctions with well-designed hierarchical structures. The novel two-dimensional (2D)/2D multi-component structure is strategically developed to eliminate agglomeration and layer stacking, thereby facilitating the insertion and desorption of ions in electrochemical processes. Meanwhile, the in-situ formed NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 heterojunction significantly diminishes charge transport resistance and boosts the electrochemical reaction rate. Besides, the incorporated NGQDs, known for their excellent conductivity and abundant active sites, further enhance the ability of electronic conductivity and charge storage dynamics of the electrode materials. The resultant composite electrode shows an impressive specific capacity of 1832.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, coupled with a commendable energy density of 68.9 Wh kg−1 when the power density is 800 W kg−1 in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//activated carbon supercapacitors. Finally, the device demonstrates superior cyclic stability, and its capacity can still be maintained at the original 91.5 % during 10,000 cycles of charge and discharge. These exemplary results underscore the potential of 2D/2D heterogeneous interfaces in revolutionizing the field of energy storage, particularly by enhancing electrical conductivity and charge storage capabilities, paving the way for developing advanced energy storage systems with optimized nanomaterial interfaces.

A novel NGQDs modified 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheet heterojunction with large interfacial contact area is developed to enhance the charge storage capacity of asymmetric supercapacitor.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The acceleration of resource scarcity and environmental pollution is a pressing issue, so the exploration of sustainable energy and electronic product storage has become an urgent concern of mankind [1,2]. Supercapacitors show outstanding electrochemical advantages among the numerous energy storage equipments due to their excellent cycling performance, high power density, and safety advantages. Electrode material modification is the key technology for further development and utilization [3–6].

Layered double hydroxides (LDH) are composed of positively charged layers and anions with intermediate equilibrium charges and have been widely used in fields such as catalysis as a layered material, energy storage, and drug delivery on account of their low material cost, excellent electrochemical properties, and easy regulation of composition [7–10]. Among them, NiCo-LDH is as a type of electrode material highly attractive and has been widely developed because of an ideal theoretical capacity, diversity of valence states, and various structures such as nanosheets, nanorods, and nanoboxes [10,11]. However, NiCo-LDH still experiences some shortcomings for its poor conductivity and insufficient active sites, leading to unsatisfactory rate performance and cycling performance [12–14]. Studies have demonstrated that constructing heterojunctions is a practical approach to compensate for shortcomings. The construction of heterojunctions can enhance the electron transfer rate between different component interfaces and regulate conductivity, hydrophilicity, chemical stability, and active site density [15,16]. In addition, there is the different energy band arrangement in different phases, which promotes the charge transfer at the interface, so that the surface electrons can modulate the heterostructure [17,18]. NiCo-LDH/transition metal sulfides, NiCo-LDH/transition metal oxides, NiCo-LDH/polymer, and NiCo-LDH/MXene have been explored supercapacitor application with enhanced rate performance, capacity, and cyclic stability [19–22].

Usually, the structure of electrode materials directly affects the active area, conductivity, and thus capacity characteristics. The two-dimensional (2D)/2D heterostructure comprises two kinds of 2D building blocks. It has intriguing electronic features in the nanoscale interface region, making it possible to effectively adjust the confinement and transmission of charge carriers and electrons [23,24]. The electrochemical properties of electrode materials can be well regulated by rational design and synthesis of 2D/2D heterostructures. For example, Chen et al. used carbon-based conductive substrate as substrate and in-situ grown 2D/2D NiCo2O4/ZnCo2O4 layered sheet composite electrode with self-supporting vertical structure. The particular structure can supply more exposed active sites to facilitate more electrochemical reactions and good electrical conductivity to facilitate the electrochemical reaction activity [25]. Sathish's group reported a synergistically in situ prepared 2D/2D Nb2C/Ti3C2 MXene hetero-interface structure of nanomaterials, presenting more contact active surfaces with the electrolyte to expand the diffusion pathway of ions into the interior. To some extent, it significantly improves the charge storage dynamics and microstructure stability [26]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are typical zero-dimensional (0D) functional graphene fragments, which are promising supercapacitor materials because of their large specific surface area, high carrier mobility and conductivity, abundant active sites, and high solubility in various solvents [27,28]. Composite dimensional composite materials with 0D and 2D semiconductor materials can trigger synergistic effects, such as spatial separation of charge carriers and further enhancing capacitance and rate capability [29]. In GQDs@NiCo-LDH composites, electron localization can be regulated owing to the difference in Fermi energy levels between these two components. At the same time, modification with GQDs can improve charge mobility, providing excellent capacitance response [30].

Moreover, the combination of Faraday and non-Faraday processes has been proven to be an effective approach for the simultaneous use of high-conductivity electric double-layer capacitors and pseudo-capacitive reactions of quasi-capacitors to enhance energy density. Herein, N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) modified NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 hierarchical heterojunction on carbon cloth were successfully prepared. The unique structure exhibits good conductivity and abundant active sites owing to the synergistic effect of the multi-components, further improving the conductivity and charge storage dynamics. Expectedly, the composite electrode delivered an excellent capacitance of 1832.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The as-fabricated CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//activated carbon supercapacitor exhibits an energy density of 68.9 W h kg−1 (at the power density of 800 W kg−1) and a superior cyclic retention of 91.5 % capacitance (10,000 charge-discharge cycles).

The carbon cloth (CC) substrate was treated with acetone, deionized water, and ethanol solution to enhance their hydrophilicity. Firstly, a solution of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.58 g) and dimethylimidazole (1.32 g) was separately dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the aforementioned dimethylimidazole solution was swiftly added to the Co(NO3)2·6H2O solution. Concurrently, the treated carbon cloth was immersed in the mixing solution and maintained at room temperature for a duration of three hours. Finally, the CC adorned with Co-MOF were extracted from the solution, subjected to sequential washing with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The Ni(NO3)2·6H₂O (0.02 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water through sonication for 30 min. The CC@Co-MOF was then immersed in Ni(NO3)2 solution and autoclaved for a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 1 h. The resulting CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 was subsequently cleaned and used for subsequent experimental operations. The preparation of NGQDs was carried out using the previously reported method [31]. The obtained CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 material was immersed in a dispersion of NGQDs (Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd) for 15 min, and the as-obtained CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs underwent thorough washing with ethanol and water. Subsequently, it was subjected to a 12-hour drying process in an oven at 60 °C.

In order to study the electrochemical behavior of the electrodes, a three-electrode system is used, which includes the working electrode (the prepared CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs), the reference electrode (Hg/HgO electrode), and the counter electrode (Pt electrode). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves, constant-current charge-discharge (GCD) curves, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests are applied to characterize the electrodes using the CHI 760E workstation in a 6 M KOH solution. In the two-electrode system, the prepared working electrode acts as the positive electrode, the activated carbon electrode is the negative electrode, and nickel foam (NF) is used as the base material. In detail, the electrodes were prepared as follows: 80 % activated carbon, 10 % acetylene black, 10 % PVDF, and a small amount of n N-Methylpyrrolidone were evenly mixed to make a paste-like slurry. The slurry was then coated on the surface of NF and dried.

Fig. 1 illustrates preparation conditions and processes of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs composites with hierarchical heterostructure. The commercial carbon cloth is initially immersed in dimethyl imidazole solution to facilitate the growth of sheet-like Co-MOFs. Subsequently, the Co-MOFs undergo an in-situ hydrothermal reaction, transforming into NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. Finally, the NGQDs was introduced under static interaction and uniformly embedded on NiCo-LDH nanosheets yielding the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs with hierarchical structures.

Firstly, the phase tracking characterization of the prepared samples was carried out by XRD characterization method to confirm the final phase formation of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs samples. The typical diffraction peaks at 26.1o and 43.3o belong to CC, the apparent diffraction peak at 11.6o, 23.2o and 35.0o are in high agreement with the crystal plane signal corresponding to NiCo-LDH (No. 40–0216), and the typical characteristic peak of Ni(OH)2 at 19.2o, 33.1o, 38.6o, 52.1o, 59.1o and 62.7° are in good correspondence with the reported diffraction peak of JCPDS No. 14-0117 proving the successful synthesis of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs samples in Fig. 2a. As a result of the relatively low proportion of NGQDs in the composite material load, no obvious peaks can be observed in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs which could be attributed to NGQDs.

A comprehensive XPS analysis was systematically undertaken to elucidate the elemental valence states of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. Four significant peaks at 284.7, 285.9, 288.0 and 289.1 eV are attributed to CC, CO, CN and O-C=O in the C 1s spectra (Fig. 2b) [32]. Nitrogen substitution of carbon atoms on the graphene plane can improve the sample's chemical and physical properties such as electrical conductivity and surface affinity for electrolytes, thus providing additional capacitance to the material. The analysis of the Ni 2p spectrum of Fig. 2b shows that the two obvious peaks of Ni 2p3/2 orbit in 855.2 eV and 872.8 eV can confirm the existence of Ni2+ ions in the sample, while the characteristic peaks in 856.9 eV and 874.5 eV belong to the corresponding peaks of Ni3+, and the other two 861.8 eV and 879.8 eV are detected satellite peaks. The fitting results of Ni spectra prove the co-existence of Ni2+ and Ni3+ in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs and suggest a partial redox reaction involving within the material [33]. The following reaction formula (Eqs. 1-3) succinctly describes the Faraday redox reaction process involved in the material [34]:
(1)
Ni
OH
2
+
OH −
↔ NiOOH +
H 2
O +
e −
(2)
Co
OH
2
+
OH −
↔ CoOOH +
H 2
O +
e −
(3)
CoOOH +
OH −
↔
CoO 2
+
H 2
O +
e −

The fitted Co 2p spectrum in Fig. 2d is well divided into Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 spin orbit parts and satellite peaks (786.9 and 803.3 eV), the binding energies of 781.1 and 796.6 eV belong to Co3+ peaks. The binding energies of 782.3 and 797.9 eV are corresponding to Co2+ peaks [35]. Additionally, the existence of ion vibration peak of Co2+ and Co3+also indicates the redox conversion between Co2+/ Co3+ in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. This co-existence indicates the improved capacity to store charge effectively. Fig. 2e depicts the O 1s fitting peak with three distinct peaks at 530.4, 531.3 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to M-O-M, M-O-H and H-O-H bonds, respectively [36]. Commonly known as M-O-M bonds signify the characteristic metal‑oxygen bond, the existence of M-O-H bond is related to the oxygen vacancy (OV) on the surface, and the key peak corresponding to H-O-H bond belongs to the water molecule species generated near the surface. The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2f) is well fitted to form three distinct vibration peaks of pyridinium N (399.1 eV), pyrrole N (399.7 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (401.5 eV). Pyrrolic nitrogen is known to supply more active centers and higher electron mobility, notably, the ratio of pyrrolic nitrogen surpasses other two nitrogen types, suggesting that the storage performance of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 can be significantly improved by using the strategy of in-situ loading of quantum dots on nanosheets [37].

SEM is employed for characterizing the microstructure and the surface morphology. Fig. 3a-c depict the images of Co-MOF precursor with nanosheets structure on carbon cloth, showcasing the progression from overall view to the local details. The length of single nanosheet is approximately 0.25 μm, which are uniformly distributed on the surface of CC. As depicted in Fig. 3d-f, following etching via hydrothermal reaction, the 2D nanosheet structural Co-MOF transforms to a 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, which exhibits a length of approximately 0.45 μm. 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 presents regular nano-sheets, which are orderly distributed on the Co-MOF skeleton. The obtained 2D/2D hierarchical structure may provide more active centers, which can effectively facilitate electron and electrolyte ion transport and significantly improve the charge storage capability of the electrode material. Assembling NGQDs onto NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets by room temperature impregnation to construct multi-component composite electrodes with hierarchical structure is helpful to enhance the comprehensive electrochemical performance of electrodes. Minor morphological changes are observed after immersion NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets in the NGQDs dispersion as illustrated in Fig. 3g-i. The main nanosheets burst open like flower petals while the adorned nanosheets remain stable and arranged in an ordered fashion on the surface. The resulting open structure offers a larger effective contact surface area. In addition, the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs nanosheets heterojunctions with 2D/2D nanostructures are mainly caused by the electrostatic interaction between NGQDs and NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2. Due to the abundance of oxygen-containing groups, the surface of NGQDs is negatively charged. Therefore, the strong interaction between NGQDs and NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 will drive the resulting 2D/2D layered structure, promoting rich interfaces and synergies between the active components that can contribute additional specific capacitance to the material.

The TEM images of NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs in Fig. 4a and 3b suggest that the composite is composed of overlapping ordered sheet-like nanosheets. From the high-power image of Fig. 4b, we can see that the ultra-thin nanosheets are adhered to the leaf-like surface in situ, which is highly in agreement with the test results of SEM. The HRTEM image of NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs in Fig. 4c shows the bladed NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheet surface with many relatively dark lattice stripes on its surface, which indicates that NGQDs quantum dots are successfully loaded on the nanosheets. The three enlarged images in Fig. 4c(i-iii) all show a regular arrangement of lattice fringes and indicate a lattice spacing length of 0.21 nm belonging to NGQDs quantum dots, aligning precisely with the (100) plane of NGQDs. Furthermore, the lattice fringes of 0.25 nm and 0.27 nm are attributed to the (012) plane of NiCo-LDH and the (100) plane of Ni(OH)2, respectively. Simultaneously, the TEM lattice fringe information exhibited a precise correlation with the XRD characterization depicted in Fig. 2a. This congruence that underscores the remarkable capacity of NGQDs to preserve the structural integrity of NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, effectively mitigating inherent instability within the crystal lattice. This integration serves to mitigate the instability of the crystal structure, resulting in the successful construction of 2D/2D hierarchical structure. To ascertain the elemental distribution within the NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs nanosheets, TEM element mapping analysis (Fig. 4d) was conducted. Ni, Co, O and N elements are evenly distributed in the composites. Significantly, the presence of N, originating from NCQDs, corroborates the successful integration of NCQDs onto the surface of NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, underscoring the comprehensive nature of the composite structure.

In the typical cyclic voltammetry curve of Fig. 5a at 2 mV s−1, clear redox peaks exist in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs, compared to the rectangular shape of the CC@Co-MOF curve. Additionally, the total area under the curves for CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs exceeds CC@Co-MOF, suggesting a substantial improvement in electrochemical properties. The CV area of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs is larger than that of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, which indicates that the incorporation of NGQDs results in a notable enhancement in electronic conductivity. The subsequent GCD curves in Fig. 5b are further used to compare the nuanced electrochemical characteristics of these three electrodes. The capacitance of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs material is determined to be 1833.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 by substituting GCD measurements into Eq. (4) [38]. By comparison, the calculated capacitance of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 is only 828.8 F g−1, while it is 409.2 F g−1 for CC@Co-MOF.
(4)
C =
i ∆ t
∆ m ∆ V
C (F g−1) corresponds to the specific capacitance provided by the sample electrode. The variable i (A) denotes the charging current, Δt (s) refers to the discharging time, Δm (g) represents the load of the active materials on the carbon fiber, and ΔV (V) indicates the discharge voltage. Besides, the positions and shapes of GCD curve platforms presented by CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 in Fig. 5b align well with the corresponding mapping of CV curves in Fig. 5a. It can be seen that CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs has nearly symmetrical GCD curve, which shows its superior reversibility. Notably, It can be noticed that the curve of the redox peak remains basically stable with the increase of scanning rate. The shapes of the redox curves still maintained despite as scanning rate increasing, as evidenced from Fig. 5c. Nevertheless, oxidation peak and reduction peak tend to shift in the opposite direction respectively, which indicates that the internal resistance of the material is polarized. The corresponding specific capacitances are calculated as 1832.8, 1772.5, 1771.4, 1734.7, 1692.8 and 1668.0 F g−1 by subjecting the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode different current densities including 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 and 10 A g−1 in Fig. 5d. Additionally, the detailed electrochemical tests of CC@Co-MOF, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, and AC are presented in Fig. S2 and 3 (supporting information). It is noticeable that the capacitance curve of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs consistently surpasses the other two, underscoring the effective enhancement of electrochemical capacitance through the introduction of NGQDs (Fig. 5e). More noteworthy is that the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode material exhibits ultra-high rate performance, with a current density increased to 10 times and a capacity still maintained at 91.0 %, higher than CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 (84.7 %) and CC@Co-MOF (72.3 %). Utilizing to design a specific equivalent circuit corresponding to the electrode material (inset in Fig. 5f), the Nyquist curves and amplification curves of CC@Co-MOF, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs are obtained to further investigation of the electron transport kinetic mechanism of the electrode material. It can be observed from Fig. 5f that the Nyquist curve contains a semicircular portion in the high frequency range and a linear part in the low frequency range with slope in the low frequency region. The intercept obtained on the X-axis, located in the high frequency part, points to the equivalent series resistance (Rs) in the equivalent circuit, representing overcoming its own material resistance. The diameter corresponding to the semicircle part is attributed to the charge transfer resistance (Rct), indicating the charge transfer ability [39–41].

Notably, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs exhibit the smallest Rct values under the comparison of the other two sample electrodes, while CC@Co-MOF demonstrates the highest values. This distinction is attributed to the layered structure of Co-MOF-derived NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2, which maximizes its exposed active specific surface area and significantly broadens the diversified path of interlayer charge express transport. Simultaneously, the judicious distribution of NGQDs on NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 nanosheets expands its effective active area to supply additional active centers and helps to improve the transport strength of electron ions.

To further determine the energy storage principle of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs, the CV curve at a lower scanning rate (2–5 mV s−1) was recorded. Obvious redox peaks with a scanning-rate-independent shape can be observed from Fig. 6a, indicating their excellent redox reversibility. The energy storage type of the electrode can be evaluated according to the above curves and the formula i = avb. In general, a and b are adjustable parameter values, and the value of b is obtained by quantifying the relationship between the peak current (i) and the scan rate v [42–44]. According to the b value, the energy storage behavior can be defined as the following three specific situations: capacitive and diffusion control process (0.5 < b < 1), diffusion-controlled electrochemical behavior (b = 0.5), and capacitive control process (b = 1). By linearly fitting its peak value in Fig. 6b, the b values are 0.7884 and 0.6877 for the anodic peak and cathodic peak, which suggest the coexistence of capacitive control and diffusion control the process in CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode. The detailed analysis of the CV curves for CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode is applied to determine the contribution ratios (Fig. 6c). The difference between capacitive and diffusion control contribution in the total charge storage process can be accurately judged by the formula i = k1v + k2v1/2, where k1v represents the proportion of current contribution from capacitive control, while the contribution rate of diffusion control corresponds to k2v1/2 [45–47], the result is shown in Fig. 6d. The capacitive contribution amounts to 26.5 % when the sampling rate of 2 mV s−1, and increased to 59.8 % at 5 mV s−1. Therefore, capacitive contribution is a major factoring the total capacity of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs electrode, which benefits the migration velocity of electrons and ions in the electrode material. Furthermore, the active area is as an important parameter determining the capacity characteristics of electrode materials. Fig. 6e and f showed the CV area of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 and CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs. The former is significantly smaller than the latter. Based on the linear regression of the capacitive current in CV curve at different scanning rates, it is determined that CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs shows a higher double layer capacitance of 0.51 mF cm−2. In comparison, the value of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 is 0.4 mF cm−2, indicating that embedding NGQDs can effectively increase the active area and promote sufficient redox reactions.

For further exploration of the practical application of the target electrodes, CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs positive electrode and activated carbon negative electrode equipped with 6 M KOH electrolyte were fabricated into ASC in Fig. 7a. The range of voltage window of supercapacitor is directly related to their energy density, therefore, first in the three-electrode system. Fig. 7b shows the CV tests of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs and AC in the three-electrode system. Obviously, the voltage windows of the two electrodes can be well matched between 0-0.5 V and-1-0 V, corresponding to the z final sample electrode CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs and electrode AC. Therefore, it can be judged that the stable potential window range of the supercapacitor can be determined in the range of 0–1.5 V. Other electrochemical test diagrams are plotted in Fig. S3 (supporting information). To further determine the suitability and stability potential window of the supercapacitor, Fig. 7c shows the CV test results obtained by changing the potential range. The shape of the CV curve of the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC device ASC is basically the same under the potential window of 0.6–1.6 V, so that the suitable and stable potential of 0–1.6 V can be further determined. It can be judged that the device can maintain good stability in the subsequent electrochemical tests at a stable potential of 1.6 V. Fig. 7d and e show the detailed CV and GCD test curves of the device combining CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC. With the increased scanning rate, the current response increases, indicating a good charge storage dynamic in the supercapacitors. Besides, the charge-discharge platform in the GCD curve is highly symmetrical, which shows good reversibility and Coulomb efficiency. According to the GCD diagram, the maximum output capacitance of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC ASC device is 193.7 F g−1. Even after applying 10 times the current density intensity (10 A g−1), the excellent capacitance of 140.0 Fg−1 can still be output, showing a good rate capability of 72.2 %.

Due to low energy density being the primary limiting factor for the application of supercapacitors, we plotted the energy density and power density of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC ASC. The fabricated device shows superior energy storage characteristics with a maximum energy density of 68.9 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1 in Fig. 8a. Moreover, even if the power density increases to 8000 W kg−1, the energy density can still maintain 49.8 Wh kg−1. Remarkably, compared with the NiCo-LDH-based electrode material supercapacitors reported in the literature, its performance is significantly better than the above reports (Table S1). For verification of the actual energy storage capability, the practical application of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC devices, a pattern composed of 1 blue and 12 white bulbs can be lighted by two devices for 20 s (Fig. 8b). Cycle stability is also an important parameter for measuring the practical application of supercapacitors. Fig. 8c is the capacity/cycle number plots of the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//AC device, tested using GCD at 10 A g−1. Obviously, only 8.5 % capacity attenuation and 91.5 % of the initial capacity are observed over 10,000 cycles, which is competitive with those reported in the literature (Table S1). At the same time, the excellent coulombic efficiency (99.7 %) shows that the charge-discharge curve of the device is almost symmetrical at high current density, which verifies its high electrochemical reversibility. Therefore, the morphology of the CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs sample after multiple cycles was characterized. The illustration on Fig. 8d shows that the NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs sample is completely attached to the surface of CC without any detachment or uneven wrapping, indicating a strong bonding between the CC substrate and active materials. From the enlarged SEM image in Fig. 8d, the sample still maintains its original 2D/2D hierarchical structure, with only small surface fragments connected. Therefore, the structural stability of the prepared electrode is an important factor in maintaining its capacity.

In summary, a novel NGQDs modified 2D/2D NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2 hierarchical structure is in situ prepared. This strategy can reduce the aggregation of 2D nanosheets and supply a large interfacial contact area for ion migration. Besides, the embedded NGQDs can improve electrode materials' overall conductivity and charge storage dynamics. Thus, the composite electrode shows a satisfactory specific capacity and rate performance. The assembled supercapacitor of CC@NiCo-LDH/Ni(OH)2/NGQDs//activated carbon presents an energy density of 68.9 W h kg−1 at 800 W kg−1 and a commendable cyclic retention of 91.5 % capacitance over 10,000 cycles. Considering its excellent electrochemical performance, such advanced novel 2D/2D heterogeneous interface optimization is expected to become a forward-looking electrode material for future supercapacitor systems.

Xiaojie Xu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Hanlin Lai: Investigation, Data curation. Huiling Lu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Pengjie Zhou: Validation, Software. Yulong Ying: Funding acquisition. Yu Liu: Writing – original draft, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52203147), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LQ22B010006) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20230551).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.112919.
